🔻Des milliers d'Albanais ont manifesté notamment dans la capitale Tirana pour la cinquième fois cette semaine, en protestation contre un projet de transformation de l'île de « Sazan » (carte) en complexe touristique de luxe, un méga-projet immobilier estimé à 1,4 mds de dollars lié à au gendre du président américain Donald Trump, Jared Kushner et à sa fille Ivanka Trump.
Ces manifestations sont attisées par des soupçons de corruption mêlant le premier ministre albanais Édi Rama aux cercles affairistes de l’entourage du pouvoir américain.

On se rappelle l’évocation de projets immobiliers de luxe concernant la bande de Gaza il n’y a pas si longtemps, et la destruction en cours par Israël d’un grande partie du Sud-Liban dont de nombreuses installations touristiques notamment entre Tyr et Naqoura n’a peut-être pas que des motivations purement politiques vis à vis de l’Iran et du Hezbollah.
S’il faut sans doute épargner le Sud-Liban de toute présence étrangère directe ou indirecte, ce n’est pas pour en substituer l’actuelle à une autre et la tournure des événements doit sauter aux yeux comme une sérieuse alerte relative à des projets bien évidemment américano-israéliens au Sud-Liban notamment sur sa zone côtière.
On ose espérer que Joseph Aoun et Nawaf Salam ne sont pas des « Edi Rama » et qu’ils sont bien conscients de ce qui peut se jouer à terme sur le territoire du Liban.
Le Sud-Liban ne doit pas devenir un terrain de jeu pour investisseurs américains et israéliens après avoir été le théâtre d’un affrontement militaire débouchant sur une entreprise préméditée de « virginisation de la terre » pour lui donner une nouvelle destination.
Si tel était le cas, tous les Libanais seraient-ils prêts à trouver un terrain d’entente au-delà des clivages claniques et communautaires pou s’unir contre ces visions colonisatrices sur le Liban à commencer par le Sud ?
Une nouvelle résistance devra t-elle se mettre en place pour que ce Sud ne soit pas offert comme butin de guerre à une coalition de prédateurs pour qui la guerre est un moyen de s’octroyer de nouvelles terres vierges.
🔻En ce début Juin 2026, prenons date qu’une nouvelle résistance libanaise pourrait bien puiser sa source à Tirana et prendre exemple sur une forte mobilisation populaire contre des projets à l’odeur sulfureuse :
« Tout est lié et il faut voir loin ! », il y a sûrement des vices cachés dans la formule « Make Lebanon Great Again » et l’Albanie est là pour ouvrir grands nos yeux sur ce qui se joue « aussi » au Liban !

ALBANIA IS NOT FOR SALE
The Kushner Resort Crisis and the Pattern Nobody Is Reporting
.

For four consecutive nights, thousands of Albanians have taken to the streets of the capital Tirana demanding the cancellation of a $4 billion luxury resort project linked to Jared Kushner and Ivanka Trump. Protesters chant "Cancel the project." Their banners read "Albania is not for sale" and "Ivanka, go home." The protests began Saturday, May 30, 2026, after developers erected barbed-wire fences blocking public access to the local beach and coastline. They have not stopped since.

The project covers Sazan, Albania's only Mediterranean island -- a formerly secret communist military base -- plus the protected coastal wetland area of Vjosa-Narta, home to flamingos, seals, and sea turtle nesting sites. Kushner has described the development as a "very high-end luxury product." It will feature 10,000 hotel rooms and villas. Ivanka Trump has spoken publicly about how she and Kushner discovered the site. "We were on a friend's boat, and we stopped for a swim," she said in a podcast interview. "We swam to the island. We went on a hike, barefoot all the way up to the top, and we were just captivated."

The Albanian government of Prime Minister Edi Rama granted "strategic investor" status to Atlantic Incubation Partners, a firm linked to Kushner's Affinity Partners fund. Heavy machinery moved into the protected coastal area last month, opening access routes, digging into the sand, clearing land among pine trees, and installing fencing. In January, 41 environmental organizations from 28 countries had already called for the suspension of the resort plans, citing threats to biodiversity. The EU warned that the project raises concerns about Albania's compliance with European conservation standards required for its ongoing EU membership bid.

When protesters rejected Rama's invitation to send a delegation of 20 people to discuss "possible solutions," he made his position clear: "Albania should not be a country that fears an extraordinary project like this one, where exceptional partners have come together to invest four billion euros. There is no chance for this investment to stop as long as I am here."

As of this writing, the crisis escalated further. Albanian anti-corruption prosecutors froze the bank accounts of Albania Land Development -- the landholding company tied to the project -- amid a widening investigation into allegedly fraudulent property titles. The Special Prosecution Against Corruption and Organized Crime ordered the preventive seizure. The asset freeze compounds pressure from the street protests, a sharp diplomatic rift with neighboring Greece, and the EU warnings.
.

THE SERBIA PATTERN
,
What is not being reported in most U.S. coverage is that this has happened before -- with the same principals, the same playbook, and the same result.

Kushner attempted an almost identical project in Belgrade, Serbia. A luxury complex was planned to replace a sprawling bombed-out military complex in the heart of the city -- a designated heritage zone whose legal protections were quietly lifted by Serbian officials to accommodate the development. Serbia's Parliament passed a special law to enable it. Then Serbia's prosecutor for organized crime charged four people, including a government minister, with abuse of office and falsifying documents to clear the way for the project. Kushner subsequently withdrew entirely.

Albania is now following an eerily identical trajectory. Special investor status granted. Land title questions emerging. Prosecutors moving. The public erupting. The pattern is not subtle.
.

WHAT THIS IS
.
The Kushner Albania deal is not a conventional foreign investment story. It is the son-in-law of the sitting President of the United States using Trump family political leverage to obtain "strategic investor" status from a small, EU-aspiring government desperate for American goodwill, to develop protected environmental land with disputed ownership, over the objections of 41 international environmental organizations, the European Union, and now the Albanian people themselves -- while anti-corruption prosecutors investigate whether the land titles underlying the entire project are fraudulent.

The protesters carrying signs that read "Albania is not for sale" are not being dramatic. The prosecutors freezing the developer's bank accounts are not being political. And the pattern established in Serbia -- where the same approach collapsed under criminal investigation -- is not a coincidence.

The project is valued at $4 billion. The island was discovered on a friend's boat. The legal protections for the wetlands were removed to accommodate the investment. The government minister who cleared the way is under investigation.

The Albanian people have seen this before. Not in Serbia. Everywhere.
.

The wave of protests in Albania 🇦🇱 shows no signs of slowing. Thousands of people took to the streets of the capital, Tirana, yesterday, marking the eighth consecutive demonstration.

The protests began over concerns about a planned $1.6 billion luxury resort development along Albania's picturesque Adriatic coast. The project would include parts of the protected Vjosa-Narta Delta and the nearby island of Sazan, an uninhabited island that once served as a secret communist military base. Environmental groups argue that the development threatens a unique ecosystem of pristine beaches, wetlands, and protected wildlife. The movement has even adopted the pink flamingo as its symbol, giving rise to the name "Flamingo Revolution."

In recent days, however, the demonstrations have evolved into something larger. Many Albanians are now expressing broader frustrations over what they see as a lack of transparency from the government, as well as long-standing concerns about corruption and political favouritism.

Some critics believe Prime Minister Edi Rama is using the project to strengthen ties with the White House 🇺🇸, as the development is being led by Affinity Partners, the investment firm founded by Jared Kushner. Ivanka Trump, Kushner's wife and President Donald Trump's daughter, has also publicly promoted the project since it was announced in 2024.

Rama, who has been in office since 2013 and has governed relatively well, has strongly defended the development. He argues that it will help transform Albania into a premier Mediterranean tourist destination, create jobs, and strengthen the country's economy. Over the past several days, he has repeatedly backed the project through social media and interviews with major international media outlets.

Having visited this part of Albania in the summer of 2024, my initial reaction was to welcome the investment as a potential economic opportunity. For a country with a relatively small economy, a project of this scale could have a significant impact. Beyond construction and supplier contracts, it could create jobs, improve infrastructure, and encourage greater international investor confidence.

Unlike neighbouring Greece 🇬🇷, Croatia 🇭🇷, and Montenegro 🇲🇪, which have long benefited from luxury coastal tourism, much of Albania's coastline remains comparatively undeveloped. That is largely a legacy of the communist era. From 1944 until the early 1990s, Albania was one of the most isolated countries in the world. Under Enver Hoxha's rule, the country severed ties with former allies, including Yugoslavia, the Soviet Union, and eventually China 🇨🇳 because the regime considered them too moderate, while an atmosphere of fear and paranoia led to the construction of hundreds of thousands of concrete bunkers across the landscape.

At the same time, I have come to better understand the concerns raised by those who oppose the project. They argue that short-term economic gains should not come at the expense of natural heritage that, once lost, can never truly be replaced.

I was particularly struck by today's article by Lea Ypi, professor of political theory at the London School of Economics and author of Indignity: A Life Reimagined, published in today's @guardian . Her argument is that self-respect must come before prosperity. The slogan "Albania is not for sale" is not necessarily a rejection of investment or development. Rather, it reflects a belief that economic growth should not require a nation to sacrifice its dignity, identity, or ability to shape its own future.

There has always been a tension between the pursuit of prosperity and the protection of values that cannot easily be measured in dollars and cents. Foreign investment can bring jobs, infrastructure, and opportunity, but it also raises an important question: what should a society be unwilling to sell? Ypi suggests that a people who trade away their autonomy, heritage, and collective self-respect for economic gain may ultimately discover that these were their most valuable assets.

Whether one agrees with the movement's conclusions or not, there is something compelling about the idea that development should come from a position of confidence rather than desperation. Prosperity and self-respect do not have to be opposing goals. In fact, the strongest and most sustainable forms of growth are often those that welcome investment while preserving the culture, environment, and traditions that make a country unique.

As someone who believes in the idea of "capitalism with a human face," I find this perspective worth reflecting on. Markets and private investment can be powerful forces for good, but they should remain connected to human dignity, community, and the public interest. Economic progress is important, but perhaps the most enduring prosperity is the kind that allows a nation to grow without losing sight of who it is.

It would be encouraging to see both the investors and the government engage meaningfully with the concerns that have been raised. If a compromise can be reached that balances economic development with environmental protection and community interests, it could serve as a valuable model for other parts of the world where local populations often find themselves at odds with government-backed foreign investment projects.
